title: Layout of Entities and Fields
breadcrumbs: Development Considerations for Performance and Scalability
Performance Guidelines for Component Logic and Implementation
text: Layout of Entities and Fields The choices you make in ordering and indexing entity fields can have a negative impact on performance. Number of Entities and Fields Painted Keep the number of entities and fields in a form, service, or report as small as possible. For each entity, a hitlist with corresponding data must be administered and each displayed field must be formatted. Minimizing Overflow Tables Uniface's maximum record length is 32 KB. If a record containing variable length fields grows to more than 32 KB, Uniface automatically creates and dynamically maintains an overflow table for the entity. Creating overflow tables has a negative impact on performance and should therefore be avoided. To avoid creating overflow tables, it can be useful to redefine variable-length fields in one of the following ways: As a short field of text As a long field in a separate entity As a short field in an existing entity plus a long field in a separate overflow entity As more occurrences of fixed length fields in a separate entity Minimizing Compound Keys Minimize or avoid the use of compound keys. Compound keys result from cascading key attributes to related entities. Causing key attributes to cascade produces keys of substantial size, expressed in bytes. The number of bytes causes an extra system load in the form of unnecessary I/O. Primary keys are also indexed, so compound keys should be avoided. Was this topic helpful? Like Dislike
